text: Good afternoon. Thank you for the opportunity to present our work. The authors have nothing to disclose.

This is an interesting case of a twenty-six-year-old male with no past medical or surgical history who initially presented with a two-day history of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and Workup with CT scan demonstrated a duodenal obstruction with dilation of the proximal duodenum and stomach. This is indicated by the red arrows. The blue arrow point to the cecum, which was noted to be in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. A contrast esophagram was done and revealed classic findings of malrotation. As shown here, there's an abnormal position of the ligament of trites The duodenum does not cross the midline and continues caudally in the right side within the abdominal cavity. The diagnosis of intestinal obstruction secondary to intestinal malrotation was made, and the patient was taken to the operating room for a laparoscopic LAD procedure.

Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes a dilated stomach and duodenum with a high-riding cecum in the right upper quadrant. Adhesions between the right colon and lateral abdominal wall are lysed using countertraction and thermal energy. The colon is reflected medially to expose the duodenum and pylorus. The duodenum is mobilized, and a partial Kocher maneuver is performed, revealing its abnormal position on the right side of the abdominal cavity. Adhesions between the colon and duodenum are lysed using sharp dissection and a harmonic scalpel. Ladd's bands are identified in the right upper quadrant, adhering the cecum to the abdominal wall. The small bowel is examined distally, and adhesions are freed, including a thick fibrous band between the small bowel and mesentery, which is lysed with sharp dissection. This band is identified as the cause of the obstruction. After adhesiolysis, the duodenum and jejunum appear unobstructed, and the mesentery is broadened.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy by ligating the mesoappendix with a harmonic scalpel and removing the appendix using two endo loops. The appendiceal stump is cauterized and closed with a PDS suture. Following the appendectomy, the cecum is positioned in the left lower quadrant, while the duodenum remains on the right side of the abdominal cavity.

Postoperatively, the patient did well. The nasogastric tube was removed on postoperative day two and was started on a clear liquid diet. On postoperative day three, the patient was advanced to regular diet without any issues and was discharged the following day and on postoperative day four without any complications. At two months follow-up, the patient continued to do well without any issues. Thank you.
